name: AMA Monopoly History
description: The "AMA Monopoly History" is a conspiracy theory alleging that the American Medical Association (AMA) has historically worked to suppress alternative medical practices and control the supply of physicians to maintain a monopoly over healthcare in the United States. Proponents argue that the AMA used its influence to shape medical education, licensing, and legislation to favor allopathic medicine (conventional medicine) and marginalize or eliminate other forms of healing, such as homeopathy, chiropractic, and osteopathy. This allegedly resulted in artificially inflated healthcare costs and limited patient choice. The theory often points to the Flexner Report of 1910, commissioned by the Carnegie Foundation, as evidence of a deliberate effort orchestrated by the AMA to standardize medical education in a way that favored allopathic institutions while leading to the closure of many alternative medical schools. Mainstream historical perspectives acknowledge the AMA's historical influence in shaping medical education and professional standards, but they attribute these actions primarily to efforts to improve the quality of medical care and protect the public from unqualified practitioners. Academics argue that the Flexner Report, while influential, was not solely responsible for the decline of alternative medical schools, citing factors such as limited resources, lack of scientific rigor, and changing societal attitudes towards medicine. Critics of the monopoly theory also point out that alternative medical practices have continued to exist and, in some cases, thrive despite the AMA's influence, and that the healthcare landscape is far more complex than a simple monopoly narrative suggests.
subtitle: Alleged AMA control over US healthcare.

claims.claim: The AMA intentionally suppressed alternative medicine.
claims.evidence: The Flexner Report of 1910 recommended closing many alternative medical schools.
claims.criticism: The Flexner Report aimed to standardize and improve medical education across all schools, regardless of their focus. Many schools closed due to inadequate resources and lack of modern scientific standards, not solely due to AMA pressure.

claims.claim: The AMA controls the supply of physicians to inflate prices.
claims.evidence: The AMA influences medical licensing and accreditation, limiting the number of doctors.
claims.criticism: Licensing and accreditation are intended to ensure competency and protect patients. The AMA's influence has decreased over time, with other organizations playing a significant role in these processes. Physician shortages exist in many areas, suggesting supply issues are not solely AMA-driven.

claims.claim: The AMA is a profit-driven organization that prioritizes its members' interests over patient care.
claims.evidence: The AMA lobbies for policies that benefit physicians financially.
claims.criticism: The AMA advocates for policies it believes will improve healthcare for both physicians and patients. Lobbying is a common practice for many professional organizations, and the AMA's actions are subject to public scrutiny.

keyFigures.name: Abraham Flexner
keyFigures.role: Author of the Flexner Report, seen by proponents as an AMA tool.

keyFigures.name: Paul Starr
keyFigures.role: Sociologist who studied the history of medicine and professionalization, offering a nuanced perspective.

keyFigures.name: Harris Coulter
keyFigures.role: Historian and proponent of the AMA monopoly theory, author of 'Divided Legacy'.

timeline.event: Medicare and Medicaid established, increasing government involvement in healthcare.
Continued debate over the role of the AMA and the influence of alternative medicine.

sources.author: Abraham Flexner

sources.title: Abraham Flexner

sources.author: Paul Starr
